title: Whakairo Taiao: Carving Our Future - Sustainable Art from the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional pūrākau of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream as the lifeblood of Ngāti Kahungunu, learning how our tūpuna used natural materials from the awa for artistic expression while maintaining its mauri. The lesson connects to local master carver Hōri Mahue whose studio overlooks the stream and incorporates traditional knowledge of harvesting harakeke, raupō, and clay from its banks sustainably.
learningObjectives: Create an original artwork using only natural materials harvested sustainably from the Mangakōtukutuku stream ecosystem
Analyze and explain how traditional Māori artistic practices from Ngāti Kahungunu maintain the mauri of natural resources
Document the creative process through digital storytelling that incorporates te reo Māori terms for materials and techniques
Present artwork to peers with explanation of cultural significance and environmental sustainability considerations

activities.title: Te Haerenga ki te Awa - Journey to the Stream
activities.description: Guided visit to Mangakōtukutuku Stream with local kaumātua to identify traditional artistic materials (harakeke, raupō, clay, pigments) and learn proper harvesting protocols according to maramataka and tikanga

activities.title: Whakapapa of Materials - Cultural Connections
activities.description: Workshop with local artist Hōri Mahue exploring traditional preparation techniques of natural materials and their significance in Ngāti Kahungunu artistry, including hands-on practice with stripping harakeke and preparing natural dyes

activities.title: Toi Auaha - Sustainable Creation
activities.description: Design and create individual artworks using only materials from the stream, applying traditional techniques while addressing a contemporary environmental theme affecting the Mangakōtukutuku catchment

activities.title: Hītori o te Mahi - Digital Documentation
activities.description: Create a digital storyboard documenting the artistic process from harvest to completion, incorporating te reo Māori terminology, cultural protocols observed, and sustainability considerations

activities.title: Whakaaturanga - Gallery Presentation
activities.description: Curate a class exhibition at the college marae where students present their artworks to whānau and community, explaining the cultural and environmental significance of their creative choices

resources: Local master carver Hōri Mahue (contact through college kaumātua liaison)
Mangakōtukutuku Stream Cultural Mapping Project digital resource
'Ngā Toi o Ngāti Kahungunu' resource book from Hastings District Library Māori collection
Harakeke harvesting toolkit provided by college (traditional cutting implements, baskets)

tasks: Completed sustainable artwork using natural materials
Digital documentation portfolio with process reflection
Oral presentation explaining cultural and environmental considerations
Peer assessment of sustainable practices used

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context - UC
Developing Practical Knowledge in the Arts - PK
Developing Ideas in the Arts - DI
Communicating and Interpreting in the Arts - CI
Level 5 Achievement Objectives for Visual Arts
